---
title: "Why 99% does not feel like 100%"
description: "The certainty effect, Allais-style choices and why guaranteed outcomes receive special treatment."
author: "Mesfin Genie"
date: "2026-07-01"
categories: [", B, e, h, a, v, i, o, u, r, a, l,  , e, c, o, n, o, m, i, c, s, ", ,,  , ", T, e, a, c, h, i, n, g, "]
page-layout: article
image: /assets/img/og-card.png
---

A small fall from certainty can feel much larger than the number suggests. The difference between 100% and 99% is one percentage point, but psychologically it can feel like moving from safety to risk.

Expected utility treats probabilities linearly: a probability multiplies utility. The Allais paradox shows why that benchmark can fail. Many people prefer a sure gain in one decision but switch toward a risky option when the common certainty component is removed. The pattern is difficult to reconcile with the independence axiom, because the shared consequence should not change the ranking.

This is the certainty effect. It helps explain why guarantees, warranties and policy commitments can matter beyond their expected monetary value. It also motivates probability weighting in prospect theory, where decision weights are not treated as the same thing as objective probabilities.

The policy implication is not that people are irrational in a dismissive sense. It is that certainty has a special role in how options are evaluated. When communicating health risks or policy benefits, a change near certainty may be perceived very differently from an equal-sized change elsewhere.

### References

Allais, M. (1953). Le comportement de l'homme rationnel devant le risque. *Econometrica*. https://doi.org/10.2307/1907921

Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk. *Econometrica*. https://doi.org/10.2307/1914185
